summary: German Enlightenment philosopher of transcendental idealism. Author of the three Critiques (Pure Reason, Practical Reason, Judgment). Developed the categorical imperative, the thing-in-itself, and synthetic a priori judgments. Never left Königsberg.

works.name: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy
works.description: Kant's central ethical framework, developed primarily in the Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals (1785). The categorical imperative demands that one act only according to maxims that could be universalized — leading to the Kingdom of Ends, where all rational beings treat each other as ends, not means. This deontological system judges actions by duty rather than consequences, producing controversial conclusions like the prohibition on lying even to a murderer at one's door. Key interlocutors include Christine Korsgaard, Schopenhauer (who attacked Kant's ethics as egoistic), and John Rawls (Kantian constructivism).

clues.clue: act only according to maxims that can be willed as a universal law
examples: only maxims that can be willed as a universal law are morally permissible
one should act only according to maxims that are universalizable

works.name: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism
works.description: Kant's magnum opus and the foundation of transcendental idealism. Argues that we only know the world through appearances, not the thing-in-itself (Ding an sich), and that space and time are pure intuitions — forms of experience, not properties of external reality. The book defends synthetic a priori truths (with '7 + 5 = 12' as the go-to example), develops the categories of understanding, and frames the entire project as a 'Copernican Revolution' in philosophy. Hume woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber,' and the Prolegomena elaborates the response. Structurally, the Transcendental Aesthetic, Transcendental Deduction, antinomies, and schematism are all frequently clued.

clues.clue: synthetic a priori propositions — truths not merely true by definition; '7 + 5 = 12' as example

works.name: Aesthetics (Critique of Judgment)
works.description: Kant's third Critique, dealing with beauty and the sublime. Beauty is analyzed through four 'moments' (including pleasure and universality), and Kant distinguishes free beauty from adherent beauty (attached to a concept). The sublime is split into noble, splendid, and terrifying varieties. Other key concepts include genius ('nature giving the rule to art'), the sensus communis (community of taste), and the four categories of subjective experience: the agreeable, the beautiful, the good, and the sublime. Hannah Arendt located an 'unwritten political philosophy' in these aesthetic writings.

works.name: Race and Anthropology
works.description: An increasingly studied dimension of Kant's work. He posited four races descending from a common ancestor, adapted a Galenic four-humor theory into a racial hierarchy (with Native Americans at the bottom), and debated race with Georg Forster. Emmanuel Eze ignited modern scholarship on Kant's racism in the 1990s. Achille Mbembe titled Critique of Black Reason and Gayatri Spivak titled Critique of Postcolonial Reason in deliberate reference to Kant. His Anthropology from a Pragmatic Point of View argued only white men possess reason and dignity. He also lectured more on geography than any other subject.

works.name: Perpetual Peace & Political Philosophy
works.description: Kant's proposal for lasting international peace, written as a mock treaty with three definitive and six preliminary articles. The first definitive article requires republican constitutions; preliminary articles ban assassins and standing armies. Kant advocates 'universal hospitality' for citizens of the world and condemns colonial 'inhospitable behavior.' The essay presages democratic peace theory. Related: the 'crooked timber of humanity' (from 'Idea of a Universal History') — 'no straight thing was ever made' — which Isaiah Berlin used as a book title.

works.name: "What is Enlightenment?"
works.description: Kant's famous essay defining Enlightenment as 'man's emergence from his self-incurred immaturity,' with the rallying motto 'Sapere aude!' (Have the courage to know!). Distinguishes public use of reason (as a scholar before the 'world of readers') from private use (in one's vocation). Notably lost its competition to Moses Mendelssohn's less-remembered entry on the same question.

works.name: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)
works.description: Kant's controversial position that lying is always morally wrong — even if a murderer asks whether your friend is hiding in your house. Benjamin Constant first challenged this implication; Kant replied in 'On a Supposed Right to Tell Lies from Benevolent Motives.' Helga Varden later added a Nazi officer to the thought experiment. Rae Langton's 'Duty and Desolation' contrasts 'sane' and 'severe' readings of Kant's position.

works.name: Neo-Kantianism & Reception
works.description: How later thinkers received and rebuilt Kant's philosophy. The Marburg school (Hermann Cohen, Ernst Cassirer) and Southwest school (Wilhelm Windelband) both rejected Kant's distinction between intuitions and concepts. The Fischer-Trendelenburg Debate introduced the 'Neglected Alternative' about Kant's theory of space. Schopenhauer both praised Kant ('a cataract operation on a blind person') and criticized him, identifying the thing-in-itself with 'will.' Modern Kantians include Korsgaard, Rawls, and Onora O'Neill. Bakhtin, Mach, and Einstein were also influenced.

Also: 'there will never be a Newton of the blade of grass,' conscience as an 'inner court,' and What is Orientation in Thinking? (intervention in the Mendelssohn-Jacobi feud).

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Common giveaway identifiers: German Enlightenment philosopher, author of the three Critiques (Pure Reason, Practical Reason, Judgment), practitioner of transcendental idealism. Never left Königsberg — his daily walks along the Pregel river were so punctual they allegedly set the city's clocks. Based at the Albertina University on the island of Kneiphof (now named for him).

comprehensive_summary:
Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) was a German Enlightenment philosopher who never left Königsberg, where his daily walks were said to set the city's clocks. He is most identified by his three Critiques and the categorical imperative.

His moral philosophy, developed in the Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals, centers on the categorical imperative: act only according to maxims that can be universalized. He derived it from four formulas (humanity, autonomy, etc.) and imagined a Kingdom of Ends where rational beings treat each other as ends, not means. His deontological ethics judges actions by duty, not consequences. The famous 'murderer at the door' thought experiment — where Kant argues lying is always wrong, even to save a friend — was challenged by Benjamin Constant, with later additions by Helga Varden (Nazi officer variant) and analysis by Rae Langton and Christine Korsgaard. Other moral concepts include radical evil (Religion Within the Bounds of Reason Alone), the shopkeeper example, and his controversial views on sex outside marriage.

The Critique of Pure Reason, his magnum opus, develops transcendental idealism: we only know appearances, not the thing-in-itself (Ding an sich). Space and time are pure intuitions (forms of experience), not properties of external reality. He argued for synthetic a priori truths (using '7 + 5 = 12' as example), proposed the categories of understanding, and framed his project as a 'Copernican Revolution' in philosophy. Hume woke him from his 'dogmatic slumber,' and the Prolegomena elaborates his response. Key structural elements include the Transcendental Aesthetic, Transcendental Deduction (which Strawson called his most important contribution), the schematism chapter, and the antinomies. The A/B edition convention began with Dilthey's Academy edition.

The Critique of Judgment covers aesthetics: beauty analyzed in four moments (pleasure, universality); free vs. adherent beauty; the sublime (noble, splendid, terrifying); taste and sensus communis; genius as 'nature giving the rule to art.' Hannah Arendt found an 'unwritten political philosophy' in Kant's aesthetics.

Kant's racial theories are increasingly studied: he posited four races from a common ancestor, adapted Galenic humors into a hierarchy, and debated Georg Forster. Emmanuel Eze ignited this scholarship in the 1990s; Achille Mbembe (Critique of Black Reason) and Gayatri Spivak (Critique of Postcolonial Reason) titled books in reference to Kant. His Anthropology from a Pragmatic Point of View argued only white men can reason. Adrian Piper's performance Food for the Spirit centered on reading Kant.

Toward Perpetual Peace proposes conditions for international peace: republican constitutions, abolition of standing armies and assassins, universal hospitality, and condemnation of colonial 'inhospitable behavior.' It presages democratic peace theory. 'What is Enlightenment?' defines it as 'man's emergence from self-incurred immaturity,' with the motto 'Sapere aude!' It distinguishes public reason (as scholar) from private reason (in one's vocation) and lost its competition to Moses Mendelssohn.

Neo-Kantianism flourished through the Marburg school (Cohen, Cassirer) and Southwest school, both rejecting Kant's intuition/concept distinction. The Fischer-Trendelenburg Debate concerned the 'Neglected Alternative.' Schopenhauer both praised Kant ('cataract operation on a blind person') and criticized him, identifying the thing-in-itself with 'will.' Modern Kantians include Korsgaard (Creating the Kingdom of Ends, Fellow Creatures), Rawls (Kantian constructivism), and Onora O'Neill (Constructions of Reason).

cards.front: Work: synthetic a priori propositions — truths not merely true by definition; '7 + 5 = 12' as example
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: thing-in-itself (Ding an sich) — unknowable objects unaltered by observation; linked to noumenon; 'two worlds' vs. 'two aspects' interpretations
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: space and time are not empirical concepts but pure intuitions / forms of experience
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: Transcendental Aesthetic — argues space and time are pure intuitions
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: categories of the understanding — 'substance,' 'causality,' etc. structure our experiences; table of judgments (quantity, quality, relation, modality)
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: Copernican Revolution — objects conform to our faculty of intuition, not the reverse
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: Hume woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber' — recounted in preface to Prolegomena
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: A and B editions — citing convention begun with Academy edition published by Dilthey
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)

cards.front: Work: antinomies — theses like 'the world has a limit in space and time' paired with antitheses; two mathematical and two dynamical
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: Transcendental Deduction — proves categories are applicable to experience; Strawson (Bounds of Sense) calls it Kant's most important contribution
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: schematism — chapter on homogeneity of rule and appearance in concepts
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: existence is not a predicate — response to ontological argument for God
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: Eckart Forster's book — systematic reconstruction arguing CPR began philosophy which ended 25 years later
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: Hume's Fork refuted — all judgments split into relations of ideas and matters of fact; Kant argues for a third kind (synthetic a priori)
cards.back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
cards.work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

cards.front: Work: four 'moments' of beauty: pleasure and universality among them
Work: beauty has 'universal validity' and is not a 'concept of the object' — judgments of beauty don't presuppose an end ('Zweck')
Work: free beauty vs. adherent beauty (attached to a set of ideas)
Work: four categories of subjective experience: agreeable, beautiful, good, and sublime
Work: the sublime — split into noble, splendid, and terrifying; Analytic of the Sublime section; Lyotard wrote 'Lessons' on it
Work: genius — 'nature giving the rule to art'
Work: sensus communis — community of taste related to two aesthetic concepts
Work: violet color example — 'soft and lovely' to one person, 'dull and faded' to another; form of finality
Work: Hannah Arendt located an 'unwritten political philosophy' in Kant's aesthetics
Work: Theodore Gracyk argued Kant believed all objects can legitimately be claimed to be beautiful
Philosopher: posited four races from a 'lineal root genus'; Galenic four-humor theory adapted into racial hierarchy with Native Americans at bottom
Philosopher: debated definition of race with Georg Forster (scientific racist)
Philosopher: Emmanuel Eze ignited study of Kant's racism in the 1990s
Philosopher: Achille Mbembe's Critique of Black Reason — titled in reference to Kant; theorizes 'Becoming Black of the world'
Philosopher: Gayatri Spivak's Critique of Postcolonial Reason — criticizes 'foreclosure' of 'native informant'
Philosopher: Pauline Kleingeld argues Kant rejected racism in later works
Philosopher: Anthropology from a Pragmatic Point of View — 'Didactic' and 'Characteristic' sections; only white men can reason and have dignity
Philosopher: Adrian Piper obsessively read and chanted Kant in performance Food for the Spirit
Philosopher: gave more lectures on geography than any other subject; sixfold division of geography
Work: three definitive and six preliminary articles for peace; written like a mock treaty
Work: first definitive article: 'the civil constitution of each state shall be Republican'
Work: elimination of assassins and abolition of standing armies among preliminary articles
Work: universal hospitality — limit rights of 'citizens of the world' to hospitality
Work: condemns 'inhospitable behavior of civilised nations' in colonial wars and slave trade
Work: presages democratic peace theory
Work: 'crooked timber of humanity' — 'no straight thing was ever made'; from 'Idea of a Universal History'; Isaiah Berlin titled essay collection after it
Work: Enlightenment = 'man's emergence from his self-incurred immaturity'
Work: motto: Sapere aude! ('Have the courage to know!')
Work: distinguishes public and private use of reason — public = as scholar before 'world of readers'; private = in one's vocation
Work: lost competition to Moses Mendelssohn's less-remembered entry
Work: criticizes 'laziness and cowardice' of men
Philosopher: lying to a murderer at your door is immoral — violates the categorical imperative / unconditionally commanding law
cards.work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

cards.front: Philosopher: Benjamin Constant's critique — if Kant's logic holds, you must tell a murderer your friend's location
cards.work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

cards.front: Philosopher: Kant's reply: 'On a Supposed Right to Tell Lies from Benevolent Motives'
cards.work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

cards.front: Philosopher: Helga Varden added a Nazi officer to the thought experiment
cards.work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

cards.front: Philosopher: Rae Langton's 'Duty and Desolation' — contrasts 'sane' and 'severe' readings
cards.work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

cards.front: Philosopher: Marburg school (Hermann Cohen, Ernst Cassirer) — rejected distinction between intuitions and concepts
Philosopher: Southwest school — also rejected intuition/concept distinction
Philosopher: Fischer-Trendelenburg Debate — 'Neglected Alternative' about Kant's theory of space
Philosopher: Hermann Cohen's Kant's Theory of Experience — anti-psychologistic reading; defends against F.A. Lange's criticisms
Philosopher: Windelband — adapted Kant into nomothetic vs. idiographic sciences
Philosopher: Schopenhauer compared reading Kant to 'a cataract operation on a blind person'; also identified thing-in-itself with 'will'
Philosopher: Lange's History of Materialism — major document in neo-Kantianism
Philosopher: Bakhtin drew on Kant in Toward a Philosophy of the Act
Philosopher: Mach and Einstein influenced by Kant's deduction that space and time are forms of experience
Philosopher: John McDowell endorsed Kant's view that raw experiences are endowed with rational content
Work: Prolegomena to Any Future Metaphysics — response to Hume; 'How is metaphysics as a science possible?'; recounts dogmatic slumber
Work: Religion Within the Bounds of Reason Alone — source of radical evil concept
Work: Observations on the Beautiful and the Sublime — early aesthetic work
Work: Physical Monadology — matter is impenetrable due to dynamic equilibrium of attractive and repulsive forces
Work: Opus Postumum — fragmentary; attempted to answer 'How is physics possible?'
Work: Metaphysical Foundations of Natural Science — embrace of Newtonian action at a distance led German scientists to reject field theories
Work: 'The End of All Things' — partly satirical philosophy of eschatology
Work: biology cannot be a precise science — 'there will never be a Newton of the blade of grass'
Work: conscience as 'inner court' of justice — examines behavior as if before a judge
Work: What is Orientation in Thinking? — intervention in Mendelssohn-Jacobi feud; 'freedom in thinking signifies subjection of reason to no laws except those it gives itself'
Philosopher: author of the three Critiques (Pure Reason, Practical Reason, Judgment)
Philosopher: never left Königsberg — daily walks set clocks for inhabitants
Philosopher: Prussian philosopher at the Albertina University on Kneiphof (island now named for him)
Philosopher: letter to Lavater condemning 'religion of ingratiation'
